inline_comments.message: We should never get any INDETERMINATE. Never during production. What is up with this?

Why the extra sleep calls? Shouldn't checking the return code be enough to know that it was pulled? I notice that we don't do enough in our scripts to check return code. Something we should probably address later.
This is a question for Tarun.  This is just fixing an install problem on the PDP-X (after consultation with Tarun) as it is expecting a default value populated here.

In case of guard decision policies, if there is a PIP DB failure the response from PDP would be indeterminate which in turn is Deny by default. In order to avoid failure for PDP Drools we give permit for guard decision policies only under indeterminate case. 
This will be removed in future once we have the Guard policies policy set implementation is done.

It seems to me that this is a reasonable approach for the time being, failure on DB (or other external components), although unusual, happens and should have a recovery treatment, regardless production or non-production environments.   

The question is what's the best default recovery treatment for this anomaly, PERMIT, DENY, or doing nothing (letting the query to PDP-X GUARD queries timeout on the querying system, in this case, PDP-D).  PERMIT or DENY are friendly.

In any case, the default value can be tweaked on the PDP-X if the default is not suitable in an installation by an operator.   

At this point in time for Control Loops integration testing with other ONAP compoments, ie. APPC, AAI, DCAE .. I think it is the most suitable value for now.
This is where an overarching platform policy should take over and return the decision. Having it configurable via seems difficult to change without rebooting the pieces.
